us-politics.headline: US Fighter Jet Shot Down Over Iran, Crew Missing
us-politics.body:
Iran shot down a US F-15 during ongoing military operations, with one crew member rescued and a search underway for a second. The incident marks a sharp escalation in US-Iran tensions and raises immediate questions about the scope and exit strategy of American military involvement in the region.

A new survey finds roughly half of Americans expect Middle East stability to worsen. Prominent voices across the political spectrum, including podcasters Joe Rogan and Theo Von, have publicly criticised the decision to strike Iran. An opinion piece in The Hill warns that a more dangerous, militarised junta is consolidating power from Iran's collapse, complicating any post-conflict settlement.

sources.title: About half expect worsening stability in Middle East: Survey
1 rescued after U.S. fighter jet shot down in Iran; search ongoing for crew member
Live updates: US rescues one F-15 crew member after Iran downs jet, searches for second
A more dangerous, repressive junta is arising out of Iran’s ashes
Rogan, Theo Von slam Trump’s move to attack Iran

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Sparks Fights Over Spending, Prisons, and Voting
us-politics.body:
The White House released its 2027 budget proposal, drawing immediate Democratic fire. House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries called it an attack on ordinary Americans' finances, while Democrats broadly labelled it 'bleak and unacceptable.' The budget eliminates LIHEAP, the low-income heating assistance programme, and proposes a $10 billion fund for Washington DC construction projects.

Trump also seeks $152 million to reopen Alcatraz as a high-security federal prison, and issued an executive order targeting mail-in voting rules. Separately, Illinois Governor Pritzker subpoenaed Trump administration officials over ICE enforcement actions. The White House dismissed the move as a political stunt. About one in four Americans now hold negative views of both major parties.

sources.title: Pritzker calls on Trump officials to testify over ICE crackdown, White House blasts move as ‘political stunt’
Trump seeks $152M to rebuild Alcatraz as ‘state-of-the-art secure prison’
Trump issues order attempting to change rules for mail-in voting
Democrats fume at White House budget request: ‘Bleak and unacceptable’
5 things to know about Trump’s 2027 defense budget proposal
Jeffries: ‘What Donald Trump has done is decimate the bank accounts of the American people’
Trump proposes $10B fund for D.C. construction, beautification projects
Trump re-ups push to eliminate low-income heating assistance program LIHEAP

us-politics.headline: DOJ Shakeup, FBI Breach, and Supreme Court Health Scare
us-politics.body:
Todd Blanche, who previously defended Trump in criminal proceedings, has taken over as the Justice Department's top official following Pam Bondi's departure as Attorney General. Fox News reports several contenders are already being floated as her permanent replacement. A judge also blocked grand jury subpoenas targeting Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, a significant check on executive pressure over monetary policy.

The FBI notified Congress of a major data breach affecting a surveillance system. Justice Samuel Alito was hospitalised for dehydration following a March event, a fact the Supreme Court confirmed only after it became public. Bipartisan senators are separately probing meetings between US officials and a Kremlin-linked delegation.

sources.title: Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
FBI labels data breach ‘major incident,’ notifies Congress
Pam Bondi is out as AG — here are the contenders who could replace her
Bipartisan senators probe Kremlin-linked delegation's meetings with US officials
Supreme Court confirms Alito fell ill at March event
Supreme Court Justice Alito treated for dehydration at hospital in previously undisclosed March 20 incident
Judge won’t reconsider decision blocking grand jury subpoenas of Fed, Jerome Powell

us-politics.headline: Immigration Arrests, ActBlue Allegations, and Army Leadership Change
us-politics.body:
Immigration officers detained the president of a Milwaukee mosque, adding to a series of high-profile ICE arrests that have drawn legal challenges and protests. The detention follows a pattern of enforcement actions in community institutions that critics say create a climate of fear, while supporters argue they reflect consistent application of immigration law.

On the political funding front, Democratic fundraising platform ActBlue faces allegations it misled Congress about the origins of foreign donations, a charge that could complicate Democratic fundraising ahead of midterms. Christopher LaNeve is set to become acting Army Chief of Staff amid ongoing military leadership transitions. Senator Fetterman requested heightened security for the NFL Draft in Pittsburgh.

sources.title: Immigration officers detain Wisconsin mosque president
Dem fundraising giant ActBlue rocked by allegations it misled Congress about foreign donations
Fetterman asks for heightened security for NFL draft in Pittsburgh
Who is Christopher LaNeve, set to become acting chief of staff of the US Army

au-politics.headline: US-Iran War Forces Albanese to Rethink Australia's Strategic Position
au-politics.body:
A US fighter jet has been downed over Iran, with its crew member rescued, as the conflict expands well beyond the Strait of Hormuz. The war is reshaping global energy flows and forcing allied governments to confront new security realities.

For Anthony Albanese, the conflict is acting as a catalyst. Analysts argue it has sharpened his focus on defence and economic reform, with questions mounting about whether the government will translate that urgency into concrete policy. Energy Minister Chris Bowen is already fielding pressure over a domestic fuel crisis tied to the broader instability.

The stakes are high heading into an election cycle. Whether Albanese moves toward what some are calling ambitious structural reform - or defaults to caution - will define the government's final pitch to voters.

sources.title: The war is no longer about just the Strait of Hormuz
Live: US crew member rescued after fighter jet downed over Iran
Energy minister Chris Bowen on the impact of the fuel crisis - podcast
Trump’s Iran war has woken Albanese up to a new reality. Will it spur him towards ‘ambitious’ reforms?

au-politics.headline: Tax Policy Divides Parties as Election Pressure Builds
au-politics.body:
Both Labor and the Coalition are scrambling to match voter expectations on tax, with the debate sharpening around whether miners should pay more. Notably, Liberal MP Andrew Hastie has signalled openness to increased resource sector contributions - a crack in traditional Coalition orthodoxy.

Albanese and opposition leader Angus Taylor are each trying to occupy credible ground on cost-of-living without alarming business or alienating workers. The gap between the parties is narrowing in rhetoric, even as their underlying positions remain distinct.

The political risk is real for both sides. Labor faces pressure to show it will act on inequality, while the Coalition must avoid looking like it protects vested interests. Neither has yet offered a fully costed position.

sources.title: Taxing times for Albanese and Taylor as parties seek to match voter expectations

au-politics.headline: Indigenous Leadership Central to Native Foods Industry Growth
au-politics.body:
Australia's native foods sector is growing but remains constrained by a structural problem - First Nations communities are often suppliers rather than owners. Industry advocates argue that without Indigenous leadership at the business level, the sector cannot reach its potential.

The argument is not just ethical. Proponents say story, cultural knowledge and community trust are core commercial assets in this market, not add-ons. Products without that grounding struggle to compete internationally.

The federal government has yet to outline a clear policy framework for expanding Indigenous ownership in the sector. Advocates are pushing for procurement rules and investment structures that shift control, not just consultation.

sources.title: Is Australia’s native foods industry missing an ingredient? ‘It’s not just about products, it’s about people, story and respect’

au-politics.headline: Queensland Man Jailed 15 Years for Live-Streamed Child Abuse
au-politics.body:
A Queensland man from North Lakes has been sentenced to 15 years in prison after live-streaming child sexual abuse. The case highlights the ongoing challenge authorities face in prosecuting online abuse that crosses international platforms and jurisdictions.

The sentence reflects a hardening judicial stance on offences involving live distribution, which prosecutors treat as aggravated due to the real-time exploitation of victims. The case drew significant law enforcement resources.

Child protection advocates say sentencing alone is insufficient without stronger platform accountability and faster cross-border cooperation between agencies.

sources.title: Queensland man jailed for 15 years after live-streaming child abuse

world.headline: US Fighter Jet Shot Down as Iran War Escalates
world.body:
Iranian forces shot down a US fighter jet and launched a search for its crew, releasing footage of American aircraft combing the area. A Red Crescent supply warehouse in southwest Iran was also struck, and drone footage showed an Israeli factory damaged by Iranian missile debris - widening the conflict's footprint across the region.

A French-owned container ship became the first to transit the Strait of Hormuz since the war began, a closely watched test of whether commercial shipping can resume through the critical chokepoint. Trump, meanwhile, told reporters the US could eventually "take the oil" in Iran, while separately requesting a record $1.5 trillion defence budget from Congress - the largest in American history.

sources.title: French-owned ship passes through Strait of Hormuz
What we know about the US fighter jet shot down in Iran
French-owned container ship transits Hormuz Strait in first since Iran war
Drone footage shows a factory in Israel damaged by Iranian missile debris

Pete Hegseth asks US Army's top general to step down

world.headline: Sudan Hospital Bombed; Israel Threatens Lebanon Infrastructure
world.body:
A drone strike killed at least 10 people at a hospital in Sudan, according to medical charity MSF. The attack is the latest in a conflict that has repeatedly targeted civilian infrastructure despite international condemnation.

In the Middle East, Israel threatened to destroy additional bridges in Lebanon as tensions there continue to mount. US unemployment fell in March despite the ongoing Iran war and broader economic uncertainty, though analysts warn the labour market data may not yet reflect the full impact of the conflict.

sources.title: Drone strike on Sudan hospital kills 10, medical charity MSF says
Israel threatens to destroy more Lebanon bridges as crisis mounts
US unemployment rate drops despite economic uncertainty and Iran war
US jobs surge unexpectedly in March despite Iran war

world.headline: Ukraine Gains Ground; Cuba to Free 2,000 Prisoners
world.body:
Ukraine says it has slowed Russian advances, reclaimed territory, and depleted Russian financial reserves, marking a cautious shift in momentum after months of grinding attrition. The assessment signals growing strain on Moscow's war effort.

Elsewhere, Cuba announced it will release more than 2,000 prisoners under mounting US pressure, a notable concession from Havana. In Europe, two Greek ministers resigned over a scandal involving fraudulent EU farming subsidies, deepening a political crisis in Athens.

sources.title: Ukraine slows enemy advances, liberates land, drains Russia’s war chest
Cuba to release more than 2,000 prisoners as US pressure mounts
Greek ministers resign over EU farming subsidy scandal

world.headline: Artemis II Leaves Earth Orbit; Cambodia Targets Scam Networks
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew captured striking images of Earth as the mission pushed beyond Earth orbit, marking a milestone in the programme's return to deep space human exploration. The crew shared what they called a "spectacular" view during the transit.

Cambodia's parliament passed new legislation targeting cybercrime scam compounds, which have forced tens of thousands of trafficked workers to run online fraud operations across Southeast Asia. The law gives authorities broader powers to prosecute organisers and dismantle the networks.

sources.title: Artemis II crew take 'spectacular' image of Earth
Cambodia parliament approves law to combat cybercrime scam rings
Watch: Artemis II's journey so far as it leaves Earth orbit... in 85 seconds

ai.headline: OpenAI Buys Talk Show as AI Media Ambitions Grow
ai.body:
OpenAI has acquired TBPN, a tech-focused talk show, adding media production to a portfolio that already stretches well beyond building AI models. The move signals a deliberate push into content distribution, giving the company a direct channel to shape how AI is discussed publicly.

Meanwhile, AI chatbots are quietly moving into clinical territory - platforms are now prescribing and refilling psychiatric medications, raising sharp questions about liability and patient safety. Separately, a startup called Moonbounce, founded by a Facebook content moderation veteran, is pitching AI-era moderation tools to platforms struggling to police machine-generated content. The week underlines how AI is rapidly colonising adjacent industries, from broadcasting to healthcare to trust and safety.

sources.title: OpenAI takes on another "side quest," buys tech-focused talk show TBPN
Chatbots are now prescribing psychiatric drugs
The Facebook insider building content moderation for the AI era

ai.headline: Solana Protocol Loses $285M in Governance Hijack
ai.body:
Drift Protocol, a decentralised exchange on Solana, was drained of $285 million after attackers deployed a fake token and seized control through a governance exploit. The attack combined social engineering with on-chain mechanics, letting the hackers push through a malicious proposal before the community could respond.

The breach is one of the largest DeFi exploits of the year and exposes a recurring weakness in token-based governance systems: speed of attack consistently outpaces speed of defence. It follows a broader pattern of sophisticated actors targeting protocol-level controls rather than smart contract bugs.

sources.title: Solana Drift Protocol drained of $285M via fake token and governance hijack

ai.headline: H.264 License Fees Jump 45x, Threatening Streaming Costs
ai.body:
Via Licensing Alliance has raised H.264 streaming license fees from $100,000 to $4.5 million, a 45-fold increase that will hit mid-size streaming platforms hardest. Large players can absorb the cost; smaller services may be forced to migrate to royalty-free codecs like AV1 or simply pass costs to subscribers.

The timing is awkward. An Italian court simultaneously ordered Netflix to refund subscribers up to €500 each for unlawful price hikes, ruling that unilateral fee increases breached consumer contracts. Together, the two stories put the economics of streaming under unusual pressure from both the infrastructure and the regulatory side at once.

sources.title: Firm boosts H.264 streaming license fees from $100k up to staggering $4.5M
Netflix must refund customers for years of price hikes, Italian court rules

ai.headline: NHS Staff Reject Palantir Tool Over Ethics Fears
ai.body:
NHS workers are refusing to use the Federated Data Platform, built by Palantir, citing ethical concerns about the company's history with surveillance and defence contracts. The resistance is grassroots and growing, with staff declining to engage with a system that cost hundreds of millions of pounds to deploy.

The standoff highlights a tension that procurement processes rarely account for: a tool can be technically functional and contractually sound while still facing rejection from the people expected to use it. Palantir's brand carries baggage that no amount of NHS branding appears to have neutralised.

sources.title: NHS staff refusing to use FDP over Palantir ethical concerns

btc.headline: Circle Faces Heat Over $285M Drift Hack Compliance Failures
btc.body:
Circle is under mounting pressure after the $285 million Drift protocol exploit, with critics accusing the stablecoin issuer of refusing to freeze stolen USDC. On-chain investigator ZachXBT went further, alleging Circle has racked up $420 million in compliance failures since 2022, painting a pattern of inaction rather than an isolated incident.

Drift, meanwhile, sent on-chain messages directly to wallets linked to the hack in an attempt to open negotiations with the attacker. The episode sharpens a long-running debate about whether centralized stablecoin issuers should act as de facto financial cops, and what liability they carry when they don't.

sources.title: Circle under fire after $285 million Drift hack over inaction to freeze stolen USDC
ZachXBT accuses Circle of $420M in 'compliance failures' since 2022
Drift sends onchain message to wallets tied to $280M exploit

btc.headline: Bitcoin Dips Below $70K as Corporate Treasury Strategies Diverge
btc.body:
Bitcoin slipped below $70,000 this week, and the pressure is exposing cracks in the corporate treasury playbook. Companies that followed MicroStrategy's accumulation model are sitting on paper losses, while a rival camp around Nakamoto-style treasury vehicles is betting on a different structure. The divergence signals that institutional conviction in BTC is real but far from uniform.

Cathie Wood offered a contrarian read, arguing Bitcoin is done with its historic 85% drawdown cycles and flagging a new price target. Futures markets are tilting bearish and volatility has cooled, suggesting the market is in a wait-and-see mode rather than a decisive breakdown.

sources.title: Crypto Biz: Bitcoin treasuries break ranks as BTC dips below $70K
Bitcoin 'done' with 85% crashes, says Cathie Wood amid new $34K target
Crypto consolidates as volatility cools and futures markets tilt bearish

btc.headline: Coinbase Trust Charter Bid Stirs Banking Backlash
btc.body:
US community banks are pushing back hard against the OCC's move to grant Coinbase a trust charter, arguing it hands a crypto firm bank-like powers without equivalent obligations. The opposition reflects a broader anxiety in traditional finance that regulators are opening doors for crypto companies faster than the rulebook can catch up.

The fight sits inside a wider regulatory churn. In Europe, Malta is clashing with ESMA over crypto oversight authority, raising questions about whether centralized EU-level regulation will squeeze out smaller member states. Both disputes point to the same tension: who gets to set the rules as crypto embeds itself in mainstream finance.

sources.title: US community banks oppose OCC's approval of Coinbase trust charter
Centralizing crypto: Why Malta’s clash with ESMA is about more than one small state

btc.headline: Ethereum Foundation Hits Staking Target as L2 Scaling Debate Heats Up
btc.body:
The Ethereum Foundation completed its staking push, reaching its 70,000 ETH target after deploying another $93 million worth of ether. The move signals a more active treasury posture from an organization historically cautious about market influence.

On the infrastructure side, Offchain Labs argued that Ethereum Layer 2 networks need more responsive fee pricing to scale to billions of users, pointing to rigid pricing models as a bottleneck. Separately, Hyperliquid cracked the top 10 in crypto derivatives volume during Q1, with Binance still leading the pack. The data suggests decentralized trading venues are gaining ground, but centralized players remain dominant.

sources.title: Ethereum Foundation stakes another $93 million ether, reaching its 70,000 ETH target
Ethereum L2s need responsive pricing to scale, says Offchain Labs
Binance led Q1 crypto derivatives as Hyperliquid cracked top 10: CoinGlass

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, caramelised fish fillets marinated in white miso, mirin, and sake then broiled until lacquered and golden
food.tip: Marinate for at least 24 hours — the longer the miso sits on the fish, the deeper the umami and the more dramatic the caramelisation under the grill

exercise.name: The Friday Ladder
exercise.description: Ascending and descending ladder of kettlebell swings and pull-ups (1+1, 2+2, up to 10+10 then back down), with no fixed rest periods — you rest only as long as it takes to transition, making it a brutal but satisfying end-of-week metabolic finisher

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity — Derek Parfit's take
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains the same object, but philosopher Derek Parfit weaponised it against the self, arguing in 'Reasons and Persons' that personal identity over time is not what matters — survival is a matter of degree, not a binary fact
learn.why: Parfit's conclusions are quietly radical: they dissolve the fear of death, undermine self-interest as a rational foundation, and have rippled through ethics, law, and AI consciousness debates — an hour with his ideas will leave you genuinely unsure who you were this morning
